list.title: SBIBank
list.description: Family banking with separate parent and child banking apps. Where single user can act in multiple roles within the app

hero.title: SBIBank
hero.description: Family banking with separate parent and child banking apps. Where single user can act in multiple roles within the app

meta.role: Led design team, art direction, product strategy, and core concepts
meta.scope: Discovery →  Core App MVP → Child App MVP →  Launch + Support
meta.team: Up to 5 designers: product and graphic

paragraphs.text: Fully native iOS and Android apps built for parents to control family spending, set limits, and handle daily banking scenarios.
paragraphs.caption: I was based at the client’s office while managing a remote team of three product designers, as well as illustrators and motion designers.

paragraphs.text: The core idea was to separate family money from personal accounts, so users could instantly understand what they needed to manage. We supported this separation visually with color zones: blue for family finances and neutral colors for personal finances.
Family owners had full control over spending, while other parent-level users could manage their own finances. Child accounts were created and controlled by the family owner.

At the same time, the bank still needed to support single users, so we designed the full banking app experience — from ordering a card to all key payment scenarios, as well as deposits, loans, and loyalty programs.
Fully native iOS and Android apps built around a savings-first experience. The app offered a focused set of features: topping up a piggy bank, completing tasks, and checking transaction history.
paragraphs.caption: We carefully considered which features would best support children’s financial literacy — without causing harm, and while gradually helping kids understand how money works.

paragraphs.text: I came up with the idea of a goal-oriented digital piggy bank. For example, a child could save for a dream toy while parents assigned small tasks and goals to help them reach it.

We found that parents were interested in this feature because it helped them learn more about their child’s preferences. They also tended to make tasks easy enough for children to access their money quickly.
On the other hand, older teens could learn how deposits work and invest their pocket money toward larger purchases. Parents had no access to a child’s piggy bank without the child’s permission.
